title: The wall you cannot beat
intro: Subthreshold swing is how many millivolts of gate you spend per 10× drop in arrivals — on the terrain, how sharply the crowd switches off as the barrier rises. Your GAA device sits at 77 mV/dec. Shape the terrain — gate length, sheet thickness — to get under 65. Then try to beat 59 mV/dec. Really, try.
explain: You hit the Boltzmann limit: kT/q·ln10 ≈ 59.6 mV/dec at room temperature. It is not geometry — it is the thermal ball crowd itself. The exp(−Eb/kT) tail means even a PERFECT gate (barrier tracking Vg one-to-one) spends 59.6 mV per decade; electrostatic imperfection only adds the ideality factor n on top. No terrain shaping beats the crowd's own statistics. This is why supply voltage stopped scaling and leakage became a zero-sum fight — and why 'steep-slope' research (tunnel FETs, negative capacitance) tries to cheat the crowd instead of the geometry.

choices: Yes — with a perfect gate, 0 mV/dec
No — 59.6 mV/dec is the crowd's own statistics

prediction.prompt: Can ANY terrain shaping switch the crowd off faster than 59.6 mV per decade at 300 K?
